concepts.title: The term structure of interest rates

beats.text: There is not one interest rate but a whole curve, mapping each maturity to the yield it earns.
The cleanest coordinate is the zero-coupon bond, the price today of one dollar paid with certainty at maturity.

visual.tex: P(t,T) = \text{price today of \$1 paid at } T
visual.note: the discount curve prices any fixed cash flow by linearity

beats.text: Yield, instantaneous forward rate, and short rate are three equivalent descriptions of the same curve.

visual.note: the short rate is the front end of the curve

beats.text: Curves are usually upward-sloping, occasionally flat, and sometimes inverted, the historically reliable recession signal.
A five-year zero-coupon bond priced at 0.8187 per dollar implies a continuously-compounded yield of four percent.
A short-rate model posits one stochastic differential equation for the instantaneous short rate under the risk-neutral measure.

visual.sub: One SDE generates the whole curve

beats.text: Every bond price is the risk-neutral expectation of a discount factor whose discounting is itself random.

visual.note: the model reduces to one question: can we compute this expectation?

beats.text: A model is affine when the bond price collapses to an exponential that is linear in the short rate.

visual.note: Vasicek, CIR and Hull-White are all affine

beats.text: Equilibrium models read off whatever curve their dynamics imply, while no-arbitrage models fit today's curve exactly.
Every model here is driven by one Brownian motion, yet real curves show roughly three independent factors.

concepts.title: Vasicek: mean reversion in its purest form

beats.text: Vasicek wrote the simplest equation that captures the central fact: rates wander but are pulled home.

visual.sub: Mean reversion in its purest form

beats.text: The short rate is an Ornstein-Uhlenbeck process, a drift pointing back toward a long-run level plus Gaussian noise.

visual.note: a is reversion speed, b the long-run mean, sigma the volatility

beats.text: Conditional on today's rate the future rate is normal, with the mean a blend of start and target.
Paths started high decay toward the long-run level at rate a while the noise keeps them wandering.
Because the rate is Gaussian, Vasicek assigns positive probability to negative rates, once a flaw, sometimes a feature.
Cox, Ingersoll and Ross kept Vasicek's drift but multiplied the noise by the square root of the rate.
As the rate approaches zero the diffusion vanishes, so the noise cannot push the rate below zero.

visual.note: volatility now scales with the level of rates

beats.text: The Feller condition states that zero is inaccessible exactly when twice ab dominates sigma squared.

visual.note: rates stay strictly positive with probability one

beats.text: CIR keeps the affine bond form, but its coefficients are built from a new constant gamma.

visual.note: the conditional law of r_T is non-central chi-squared

beats.text: Vasicek's symmetric Gaussian can spill below zero, while CIR's right-skewed density is pinned at the floor.
Equilibrium models generally miss the curve quoted this morning, which is unusable for a desk hedging real bonds.
Hull and White made the drift's target a deterministic function of time rather than a constant.

visual.note: a whole function, not a number, fits today's curve perfectly

beats.text: The function theta is not guessed but read off the market forward curve so bond prices match.

visual.note: the last term is the same convexity correction as Vasicek

beats.text: The model zoo is a menu of compromises across tractability, realism, and exact fit to the market.
In 2026 one-factor Gaussian models remain the default for vanilla rate derivatives because they calibrate cleanly.

visual.label: driving Brownian factor in this chapter
